steps.content: Python has two main numeric types: integers (whole numbers) and floats (numbers with a decimal point). Understanding how Python handles numbers is essential for almost every program you'll write.
An integer is a whole number — positive, negative, or zero — without a decimal point. Use type() to check the type of any value.
A float is a number with a decimal point. Even a number like 7.0 is a float, not an integer.
Python supports all the standard math operators: + (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), and / (division).

a = 10
b = 3
print(a + b)
print(a - b)
print(a * b)
print(a / b)
Notice that 10 / 3 returns 3.333... (a float), even though both numbers are integers. In Python 3, division always returns a float. Use // if you want integer division instead.
Python has three extra operators: // (floor division — divides and rounds down), % (modulo — returns the remainder), and ** (exponent — raises to a power).
The modulo operator % is great for checking if a number is even or odd: number % 2 == 0 means the number is even.
Python follows standard math rules (PEMDAS): Parentheses first, then Exponents, then Multiplication/Division, then Addition/Subtraction. Use parentheses to make your intent clear.
